### Essay: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s Understanding of Formal Number in Neoplatonism and its Influence on Renaissance Thought Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s *900 Theses* reflects his deep engagement with Neoplatonic thought, especially concerning the nature and significance of numbers. Central to Pico’s view is the distinction between *formal numbers*—those that represent metaphysical, divine principles—and *material numbers*, which are limited to the physical realm and practical applications, such as those used by merchants in commerce. This distinction is a critical theme in his work, which he extends to broader metaphysical concerns, Christian theology, and the pursuit of prophecy and divine knowledge. Pico’s understanding of formal number influenced key Renaissance thinkers such as Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa and John Dee and contributed to the development of Christian Cabalistic traditions. This essay explores Pico’s understanding of formal numbers, how they contrast with material numbers, and their significance in Neoplatonism, Kabbalistic thought, and Renaissance Christian theology. It also examines how his theories on number, cited directly from the *900 Theses*, influenced later intellectual traditions. ### Formal vs. Material Numbers: Pico’s Distinction In Conclusion II.7.1 , Pico asserts that *"Mathematicae non sunt verae scientiae"*, or *"Mathematics is not a true science."* This provocative statement reveals his underlying skepticism about the value of mathematics when it is treated merely as a quantitative tool. For Pico, material numbers, which are used in commerce and practical matters, fall short of engaging with the true metaphysical realities of the universe. This reflects the Neoplatonic concern with ascending beyond the physical world into the realm of higher, intelligible forms. Pico expands this critique in Conclusion II.7.2 , where he states, *"Si foelicitas sit in speculativa perfectione: mathematicae non faciunt ad foelicitatem,"* or *"If happiness lies in speculative perfection, mathematics does not contribute to happiness."* Here, Pico reflects the Platonic idea that the highest good—*eudaimonia* or happiness—comes from contemplating the divine and the One, not from engaging with material numbers. In Neoplatonism, true happiness comes from the soul’s ascent to the realm of the intelligible, where formal numbers—the metaphysical principles that structure the universe—reside. Mathematics, if limited to material application, cannot bring the soul to this state of perfection. ### The Role of Formal Number in Neoplatonism Pico’s understanding of formal number is deeply rooted in Neoplatonism, where numbers are seen as more than mere quantities; they are symbolic of divine order and harmony. In Conclusion II.7.4 , Pico elaborates on this distinction: *"Sicut subiecta mathematicorum si absolute accipiantur: intellectum nihil perficiunt: ita si ut imagines accipiantur superiorum: immediate nos ad intelligibilium speculationem manu ducunt,"* or *"Just as the subjects of mathematics, if taken absolutely, do not perfect the intellect, so, if taken as images of higher things, they immediately lead us by the hand to the contemplation of the intelligibles."* This statement captures Pico’s belief that mathematics, when understood in its higher, symbolic form, can lead the mind towards the contemplation of divine truths. This mirrors the Neoplatonic idea that mathematical ratios and geometrical forms reflect the divine structure of reality. In the same way that Plato in the *Timaeus* used geometry to describe the cosmos, Pico suggests that formal numbers serve as images of higher realities that can guide the soul toward understanding the divine. ### Formal Numbers as a Path to Prophecy and Divine Knowledge Pico’s conclusions also point to the significance of formal numbers in gaining prophetic insight. In Conclusion II.7.9 , he writes, *"Per arithmeticam non materialem: Sed formalem habetur optima via ad prophetiam naturalem,"* or *"Through formal, not material, arithmetic, one finds the best way to natural prophecy."* This reflects the Pythagorean and Kabbalistic view that numbers are not just abstract symbols but can reveal the hidden structure of the cosmos and the divine will. By contemplating formal numbers, one gains access to prophetic knowledge—insight into the natural world and divine truths. Pico further links formal numbers to the Christian tradition of prophecy by referencing Joachim of Fiore, a Christian mystic known for his apocalyptic visions. In Conclusion II.7.10 , he writes, *"Ioachin in prophetiis suis alia via non processit quam per numeros formales,"* or *"Joachim [of Fiore] in his prophecies proceeded by no other way than through formal numbers."* For Pico, Joachim’s prophetic vision was based on a deep understanding of formal numbers, which structured the progression of history and divine revelation. This aligns with Pico’s own belief that formal numbers provide access to divine knowledge and insight into the mysteries of the universe. ### Pico’s Critique of Material Numbers Pico’s critique of material numbers is most evident in his warnings against the overemphasis on mathematics in theology and natural philosophy. In Conclusion II.7.5 , he critiques those who apply mathematics to natural philosophy, stating that *"modernos qui de naturalibus mathematice disputant naturalis philosophiae fundamenta destruere,"* or *"those moderns who discuss natural things mathematically, thereby destroying the foundations of natural philosophy."* Here, Pico argues that treating natural phenomena purely in mathematical terms reduces them to quantitative measures, ignoring the qualitative and metaphysical dimensions that are essential for true philosophical inquiry. In Conclusion II.7.6 , he goes further, stating that *"Nihil magis nocivum theologo quam frequens et assidua in mathematicis Euclidis exercitatio,"* or *"Nothing is more harmful to a theologian than frequent and constant exercise in Euclidean mathematics."* Pico warns that theologians, whose task is to contemplate divine truths, should not become too absorbed in abstract geometry and mathematical speculation, as it distracts from the higher goal of spiritual contemplation. This aligns with the Platonic idea that the material world, including material numbers, can be a distraction from the true contemplation of the Forms and the divine. ### Influence on Agrippa, Dee, and Christian Kabbalism Pico’s theories on formal number had a significant influence on later thinkers, particularly those associated with Christian Kabbalism and Renaissance magic. Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, in his *Three Books of Occult Philosophy*, expands on Pico’s ideas, drawing on both Neoplatonic and Kabbalistic number symbolism to create a system of magical correspondences. Like Pico, Agrippa saw formal numbers as having intrinsic metaphysical power and believed that by understanding the structure of numbers, one could influence the material world through magic. John Dee, another influential Renaissance thinker, was similarly influenced by Pico’s understanding of number. Dee, who was both a mathematician and a mystic, saw numbers as the foundation of all knowledge. His *Monas Hieroglyphica* encapsulates this belief, presenting a symbol that represents the unity of all things through numerical harmony. Dee’s understanding of formal numbers as divine principles, capable of bridging the material and spiritual realms, is a direct extension of Pico’s vision. Pico’s influence also extended to the Christian Kabbalistic tradition, which sought to integrate Jewish mystical traditions with Christian theology. In his *Heptaplus*, Pico engages with the Kabbalistic sefirot, which are ten emanations of divine power and structure, and uses the framework of numbers to explore the metaphysical structure of the universe. His integration of Kabbalistic number symbolism into Christian thought had a lasting impact on Renaissance mysticism and the development of Christian Cabalism. ### Conclusion Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s understanding of formal numbers reflects his broader project of reconciling Platonic, Aristotelian, and Kabbalistic traditions within Christian theology. His distinction between formal and material numbers allowed him to critique the limitations of mathematics while also acknowledging its value as a symbolic and metaphysical tool. For Pico, formal numbers were not merely abstract quantities but divine principles that revealed the structure of the cosmos and provided a path to prophetic knowledge and spiritual ascent. Pico’s theories on formal numbers had a profound influence on Renaissance thought, particularly on figures like Agrippa and Dee, who expanded on his ideas in the context of Christian mysticism and magic. By elevating formal numbers to the status of divine principles, Pico contributed to the development of a philosophical and mystical tradition that viewed numbers as both symbolic and real, capable of unlocking the deepest mysteries of the universe. Through his synthesis of Neoplatonic metaphysics, Kabbalistic symbolism, and Christian theology, Pico della Mirandola laid the groundwork for a new understanding of number that would resonate for centuries, influencing the course of Renaissance philosophy, magic, and mysticism.

Here is a translation of the list of *Questiones* from Pico della Mirandola’s conclusions, followed by explanations of the vocabulary and grammar, as well as an exploration of their philosophical context: ### Translation 1. Utrum sit deus Whether God exists. 2. Utrum sit infinitus Whether God is infinite. 3. Utrum sit causa omnium rerum Whether God is the cause of all things. 4. Utrum sit simplicissimus Whether God is utterly simple (without composition). 5. Utrum sit intelligens Whether God is intelligent. 6. Quomodo deus intelligat How God understands. 7. An sit dare naturam superiorem natura intellectuali Whether there exists a nature superior to the intellectual nature. 8. An esse quiditativum rei sit intimius esse quod habeat res Whether the quiddity (whatness) of a thing is more intimate than its existence. 9. Quid de humanitate in sua precisissima abstractione possit praedicari: et quid non What can and cannot be predicated about humanity in its most precise abstraction. 10. Quomodo elementa sint in caelo How the elements exist in the heavens. 11. Quis modus debeat teneri in investigatione uniuscuiusque scibilis What method should be followed in the investigation of each knowable thing. 12. Utrum supra naturam rerum corporalium sit dare naturam rationalem incorpoream Whether, above the nature of corporeal things, there is a rational incorporeal nature. 13. Utrum supra naturam rationalem sit dare naturam intellectualem Whether there is an intellectual nature superior to the rational nature. 14. Utrum inter naturam rationalem et intellectualem sit aliqua natura media Whether there is an intermediate nature between the rational and intellectual natures. 15. Utrum inter naturam intellectualem et deum sit aliqua natura media Whether there is an intermediate nature between the intellectual nature and God. 16. Utrum in aliqua natura contradictoria se compatiantur Whether contradictory attributes can coexist in any nature. 17. Utrum in aliqua natura coincidant contradictoria Whether contradictory attributes coincide in any nature. 18. Quis numerus annorum sit naturaliter debitus vitae boni hominis What is the natural number of years due to the life of a good person. 19. Quis sit numerus annorum naturaliter debitus vitae mali hominis What is the natural number of years due to the life of a bad person. 20. Quot sint gradus principales naturarum universi How many principal grades of natures exist in the universe. 21. Utrum natura corporea ut talis sit activa vel tamen sit passiva Whether corporeal nature as such is active or passive. 22. Quid dicat corpus What does "body" mean. 23. Utrum sint in materia dimensiones interminate Whether there are infinite dimensions in matter. 24. Utrum sit melius deum causare res quam non causare Whether it is better for God to cause things than not to cause them. 25. Utrum creatio rerum ad extra: procedat necessario a divina essentia in tribus personis hypostatizata Whether the creation of external things proceeds necessarily from the divine essence hypostatized in three persons. 26. Utrum inter causam et causatum necessario mediet aliquid Whether there must necessarily be something that mediates between the cause and the caused. 27. Utrum recte multitudo scibilium ad decem praedicamentorum numerum sit reducta Whether the multiplicity of knowable things has been correctly reduced to the number of ten categories. 28. Quae sit differentia inter modum intelligendi angelorum: et animarum rationalium What is the difference between the mode of understanding of angels and rational souls. 29. Quae sit differentia inter modum intelligendi dei et angelorum What is the difference between the mode of understanding of God and of angels. 30. Utrum natura angelica sit quodammodo omnia Whether the angelic nature is, in some way, all things. 31. Utrum natura rationalis sit quodammodo omnia Whether the rational nature is, in some way, all things. 32. Utrum possint esse plures dei Whether there can be more than one God. 33. Utrum detur infinitum in natura Whether there is infinity in nature. 34. Quomodo differat infinitas quam theologi attribuunt deo: ab illa quam dicunt philosophi impossibile esse dari How the infinity attributed to God by theologians differs from that which philosophers say is impossible. 35. Utrum deus sit omnia in omnibus Whether God is all things in all things. 36. Quomodo differat esse creaturarum ab esse dei How the existence of creatures differs from the existence of God. 37. Quae pertineant ad considerationem theologi What pertains to the theologian’s consideration. 38. Quando futura sit saeculi consumatio When the end of the world will come. 39. Quis et qualis erit rerum status in saeculi consumatione What and of what kind will be the state of things at the end of the world. 40. Quae opinio verior de trinitate Arrii, Sabellii, Eucliph, aut fidei catholicae Which opinion of the Trinity is truer: that of Arius, Sabellius, Euclides, or the Catholic faith. 41. Utrum formae sensibiles sint intelligibiliter in angelo Whether sensible forms exist intelligibly in an angel. 42. Utrum in angelo sit aliud quam intelligens, intellectio et intellectum Whether there is in an angel anything besides the knower, the act of knowing, and the known. 43. Utrum sit eadem natura moventis et moti motu physico Whether the nature of the mover and the moved is the same in physical motion. 45. Utrum sit materia eiusdem rationis in omnibus Whether matter is of the same kind in all things. 49. Certainly! Here’s the continuation of the translation starting from question 60: 60. Quare solum de inherentia passionis ad subiectum habetur scientia Why is there knowledge only of the inherence of passion in the subject. 61. Utrum anima rationalis sit materialis Whether the rational soul is material. 62. Utrum sit incorruptibilis Whether the rational soul is incorruptible. 63. Utrum universaliter intelligat Whether the rational soul understands universally. 64. Utrum sicut est dare mundum sensibilem: ita sit dare mundum intelligibilem Whether, just as there is a sensible world, there is also an intelligible world. 65. Utrum sit maior vel minor numerus separatarum specierum quam materialium Whether the number of separate species is greater or lesser than the number of material species. 66. Quare in opere secundae diei non est dictum: Et vidit deus quoniam bonum Why it is not said in the work of the second day, “And God saw that it was good.” 67. Quare sexta numeratio homo dicitur Why man is said to be the sixth number. 68. Quare sex diebus dicitur deus omnia perfecisse Why it is said that God completed all things in six days. 69. Quid significet deum septima die quievisse What it means that God rested on the seventh day. 70. Utrum distinguatur irascibilis a concupiscibili Whether the irascible part is distinguished from the concupiscible part of the soul. 71. Quae sit distinctio inter potentias animae cognoscitivas What is the distinction between the cognitive powers of the soul. 72. Cur homines naturaliter appetant victoriam Why human beings naturally desire victory. 73. Cur naturalis sit homini modus cognoscendi per rationem phantasiae coniunctam Why it is natural for human beings to know by a mode combining reason and imagination. 74. Utrum in caelo sint descripta et significata omnia cuilibet scienti legere Whether everything is written and signified in the heavens for anyone who knows how to read. --- ### Explanations of Vocabulary and Context: - Utrum – This word introduces a yes/no question. Pico is using it to pose fundamental metaphysical and theological questions. - Intelligibilis – Refers to things that can be understood by the intellect, often used in contrast to "sensibilis," or things perceivable by the senses. Pico’s framework heavily draws on Neoplatonic and Aristotelian ideas of intellect and intelligibility. - Materiam primam – "Prime matter" refers to the concept in Aristotelian philosophy of an underlying, formless substance from which all physical forms are derived. In this context, Pico explores how material reality relates to metaphysical realities like God and the soul. - Incorporeus – Means "without a body." Pico’s questions on the nature of intellectual and incorporeal beings show his engagement with Platonic and Aristotelian metaphysics, particularly the hierarchy of being. - Actus et potentia – A reference to Aristotle’s distinction between "actuality" and "potentiality," key concepts in both Aristotelian and Scholastic philosophy. This is central to Pico’s metaphysical investigations about how creatures exist in relation to God. - Praedicatio – "Predication" refers to the way something is affirmed or denied of a subject in a proposition. Pico’s investigation into modes of predication reflects his interest in logic and metaphysics, particularly how we speak about divine and created realities. ### Philosophical Context: Pico’s *Questiones* show his deep engagement with metaphysical and theological issues that were central to medieval and Renaissance thought, especially in the context of reconciling Platonism, Aristotelianism, and Christian doctrine. Many of these questions reflect themes in Christian Neoplatonism, such as the distinction between the material and the immaterial, the nature of God’s relationship to creation, and the hierarchical structure of the cosmos. For example, questions 1–5 concern the nature of God—His existence, infinity, simplicity, and intelligence. These questions echo centuries of theological inquiry, from Augustine and Aquinas to Pico’s contemporary Marsilio Ficino, but Pico also introduces innovative questions about the relationship between God’s intellect and being, such as question 6 on "how God understands." In questions 7–15 , Pico delves into questions about the hierarchy of being, including the existence of intermediate natures between the rational, intellectual, and divine realms. This shows his deep engagement with Neoplatonism, particularly the idea that there are gradations of reality, with the material world at the bottom and God at the top. This reflects his interest in bridging Platonism with Kabbalistic ideas, where emanations from God (the sefirot) represent different levels of divine reality. Questions 16–17 on the coexistence or coincidence of contradictions reflect Pico’s fascination with the paradoxes of existence, likely influenced by Nicholas of Cusa’s ideas about the "coincidence of opposites," a theme that is important in his *Oration on the Dignity of Man*. The questions on number (such as 18, 19, and 20) reveal Pico’s understanding of formal numbers as metaphysical realities, which goes beyond the mundane arithmetic of "merchants" and into the symbolic and mystical dimensions of number. These questions tie into his later influence on thinkers like Agrippa and John Dee, who would explore numbers as tools for understanding divine order and magic. Overall, this set of *Questiones* reveals Pico’s ambition to integrate diverse intellectual traditions—Christianity, Neoplatonism, Aristotelianism, Kabbalah—into a grand synthesis of metaphysical and theological knowledge. Each question opens a door to a complex web of philosophical problems, many of which remained central to Renaissance and early modern thought.

### II.7a.60 Conclusion : *Quare solum de inherentia passionis ad subiectum habetur scientia.* Translation : *Why is knowledge only about the inherence of passion in the subject?* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Quare – Why - solum – only - de – about - inherentia – inherence (genitive singular of *inherentia*) - passionis – of passion (genitive singular of *passio*) - ad – towards/about - subiectum – subject (accusative singular of *subiectum*) - habetur – is held/considered (passive form of *habeo*) - scientia – knowledge (nominative singular) The sentence is a yes/no interrogative structure introduced by *Quare* (why), questioning the scope of knowledge in relation to passions inherent in subjects. Philosophical Context : Pico is addressing the nature and scope of human knowledge, specifically questioning why knowledge is confined to understanding the inherent passions within subjects. This reflects broader Renaissance and Neoplatonic inquiries into the limits of human cognition and the relationship between emotions (*passiones*) and intellect (*intellectus*). Originality or Paraphrase : This conclusion appears to be original to Pico, as it combines his unique synthesis of Neoplatonic thought with Christian theological concerns. While the interplay between emotion and reason is a common theme in philosophical discourse, Pico's specific framing—focusing on the inherence of passion as the sole domain of knowledge—shows his individual approach. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion diverges from the more conventional Scholastic view, which often seeks to harmonize emotion and reason as complementary rather than limiting. By asserting that knowledge is only about inherent passions, Pico challenges the idea that reason alone can fully comprehend the subject, suggesting a more integrated or even hierarchical relationship between emotion and intellect. --- ### II.7a.61 Conclusion : *Utrum anima rationalis sit materialis.* Translation : *Whether the rational soul is material.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - anima – soul (nominative singular) - rationalis – rational (adjective modifying *anima*) - sit – is (subjunctive mood, used in indirect questions) - materialis – material (predicate adjective) This is a yes/no interrogative questioning the nature of the rational soul. Philosophical Context : Pico is delving into the metaphysics of the soul , a central topic in both Aristotelian and Neoplatonic traditions. The debate centers on whether the soul possesses a material essence or is entirely immaterial , reflecting deeper questions about the nature of consciousness and existence. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is paraphrased from longstanding philosophical debates, notably those between Plato (who posited an immaterial soul) and Aristotle (who introduced hylomorphism, suggesting the soul as the form of the body). Pico's framing is typical of Scholastic inquiries but his conclusions often incorporate Neoplatonic nuances. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion likely aligns with the conventional Scholastic and Neoplatonic view that the rational soul is immaterial . This stance supports the idea of the soul's immortality and its capacity to engage with the intelligible realm, consistent with thinkers like Aquinas and Proclus . --- ### II.7a.62 Conclusion : *Utrum sit incorruptibilis.* Translation : *Whether it is incorruptible.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - sit – is (subjunctive mood) - incorruptibilis – incorruptible (predicate adjective) This is a yes/no interrogative concerning the incorruptibility of the subject, likely the rational soul. Philosophical Context : Pico is questioning whether the rational soul can be incorruptible , a key attribute in discussions about the soul's immortality and its separation from the physical body. This ties into Neoplatonic beliefs about the soul's eternal nature and its ability to transcend physical decay. Originality or Paraphrase : The question echoes traditional philosophical debates, particularly those in Christian Scholasticism and Neoplatonism , about the soul's incorruptibility. However, Pico may introduce original nuances in his reasoning or implications. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's stance likely conforms to the conventional view that the rational soul is incorruptible , aligning with Scholastic and Neoplatonic thought that affirms the soul's eternal and unchangeable nature. --- ### II.7a.63 Conclusion : *Utrum universaliter intelligat.* Translation : *Whether it understands universally.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - universaliter – universally (adverb) - intelligat – understands (subjunctive mood) This is a yes/no interrogative about the scope of understanding. Philosophical Context : Pico is probing the capacity of universal understanding within the rational soul. This relates to debates on universal cognition —whether the soul can comprehend universal truths or concepts independently of particular instances. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original in its specific inquiry but paraphrases broader philosophical themes present in Platonism and Scholasticism concerning the universality of knowledge and understanding. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico likely supports the conventional Scholastic view that the rational soul possesses the capacity for universal understanding, in line with Aquinas and Proclus , who argue that the intellect can grasp universal forms or ideas. --- ### II.7a.64 Conclusion : *Utrum sicut est dare mundum sensibilem: ita sit dare mundum intelligibilem.* Translation : *Whether, just as there is to give a sensible world, there is also to give an intelligible world.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - sicut – just as - est dare – there is to give (act of creation) - mundum sensibilem – a sensible (material) world - ita sit dare – similarly, there is to give - mundum intelligibilem – an intelligible (abstract) world This is a yes/no comparative interrogative about the creation of different types of worlds. Philosophical Context : Pico is exploring the possibility of a parallel creation —the existence of both a sensible (material) and an intelligible (abstract) world. This reflects Neoplatonic dualism, where the intelligible realm exists alongside the material, each with distinct characteristics and origins. Originality or Paraphrase : The concept paraphrases Neoplatonic ideas but also introduces original synthesis by contemplating the simultaneous creation of both realms within a Christian framework. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion aligns with the Neoplatonic and Christian Scholastic view that an intelligible world exists as a higher, abstract reality alongside the material world. This dual creation underscores the hierarchy and interconnectedness of different realms of existence. --- ### II.7a.65 Conclusion : *Utrum sit maior vel minor numerus separatarum specierum quam materialium.* Translation : *Whether the number of separate species is greater or lesser than that of material species.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - sit – is - maior – greater - vel – or - minor – lesser - numerus – number - separatarum specierum – separate species (genitive plural of *separata species*) - quam – than - materialium – material (genitive plural of *materialis*) This is a yes/no comparative interrogative comparing the quantity of separate versus material species. Philosophical Context : Pico is examining the diversity of species within the intelligible versus the material realms. This touches upon Neoplatonic and Scholastic ideas about the hierarchy of beings, where the intelligible world encompasses a broader array of forms or species compared to the limited material world. Originality or Paraphrase : The question paraphrases existing philosophical debates about the plurality of forms but does so in an original context , integrating it into his broader metaphysical system. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico likely supports the conventional Neoplatonic view that the intelligible realm contains a greater number of species due to its abstract and comprehensive nature, in contrast to the more constrained diversity of the material world. --- ### II.7a.66 Conclusion : *Quare in opere secundae diei non est dictum. Et vidit deus quoniam bonum.* Translation : *Why in the work of the second day it is not said, "And God saw that it was good."* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Quare – Why - in opere secundae diei – in the work of the second day - non est dictum – it is not said - Et vidit deus quoniam bonum – "And God saw that it was good." This is a yes/no interrogative questioning the omission of a specific biblical statement on the second day of creation. Philosophical Context : Pico is analyzing the Biblical creation narrative , specifically the structure and wording of Genesis. By questioning why certain declarations (like God's approval) are absent on the second day, Pico may be exploring deeper theological and metaphysical implications of the creation process. Originality or Paraphrase : This is an original theological inquiry, reflecting Pico's exegesis of scripture through a philosophical lens , rather than a paraphrase of existing philosophical texts. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's approach is aligned with Scholastic methods that scrutinize Biblical texts for hidden meanings or philosophical truths . However, his specific focus on the omission suggests an innovative attempt to uncover or interpret nuanced theological points. --- ### II.7a.67 Conclusion : *Quare sexta numeratio homo dicitur.* Translation : *Why man is called the sixth number.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Quare – Why - sexta numeratio – sixth numbering - homo – man (nominative singular) - dicitur – is called This is a yes/no interrogative about the symbolic association of man with the number six. Philosophical Context : In Pythagorean and Neoplatonic traditions, numbers hold symbolic and metaphysical significance . Pico is likely exploring the symbolism behind assigning man to the number six, which could relate to ideas about humanity's place in the cosmos, the structure of the soul, or other metaphysical constructs. Originality or Paraphrase : This question is original in Pico's specific symbolic interpretation but is rooted in established numerological traditions that associate numbers with cosmic and metaphysical principles. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion likely expands on the conventional view by attributing specific symbolic meanings to the number six in relation to man, a perspective that is consistent with esoteric traditions but may extend beyond mainstream Scholastic thought. --- ### II.7a.68 Conclusion : *Quare sex diebus dicitur deus omnia perfecisse.* Translation : *Why it is said that God completed all things in six days.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Quare – Why - sex diebus – six days - dicitur – it is said - deus omnia perfecisse – that God completed all things This is a yes/no interrogative questioning the theological and symbolic reasons behind the six-day creation narrative. Philosophical Context : Pico is investigating the theological significance of the six-day creation in Genesis, possibly linking it to numerological symbolism , cosmic order , and the hierarchical structure of creation as understood in Neoplatonic and Scholastic traditions. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original in Pico's specific focus but draws on established theological and philosophical analyses of the Biblical creation narrative . Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion aligns with the conventional Christian Scholastic view that the six-day creation represents a perfect and ordered process . He may further interpret these days as symbolic of metaphysical principles governing the cosmos. --- ### II.7a.69 Conclusion : *Quid significet deum septima die quievisse.* Translation : *What does it mean that God rested on the seventh day.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Quid – What - significet – does it mean - deum – that God - septima die – on the seventh day - quievisse – rested This is an interrogative questioning the theological and symbolic implications of God's rest on the seventh day. Philosophical Context : Pico is exploring the symbolism and theological meaning behind God's rest, which could signify completion , perfection , or the establishment of the Sabbath as a sacred institution. This aligns with Neoplatonic and Scholastic interpretations that find deeper meanings in Biblical narratives. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original , reflecting Pico's theological analysis rather than a paraphrase of existing philosophical doctrines. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's analysis likely supports the conventional theological view that God's rest symbolizes the completion of creation and serves as a model for human rest and worship . He may also link it to metaphysical concepts of perfection and order . --- ### II.7a.70 Conclusion : *Utrum distinguatur irascibilis a concupiscibili.* Translation : *Whether the irascible is distinguished from the concupiscible.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - distinguatur – is distinguished - irascibilis – irascible (pertaining to anger) - a – from - concupiscibili – concupiscible (pertaining to desire) This is a yes/no interrogative about the distinction between two parts of the soul. Philosophical Context : Drawing from Aristotelian psychology, Pico is examining whether the irascible (associated with anger and courage) is distinct from the concupiscible (associated with desires and appetites). This reflects broader discussions in Scholasticism about the multifaceted nature of the soul . Originality or Paraphrase : The question paraphrases Aristotle's division of the soul but is original in Pico's specific exploration within his broader metaphysical system. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion likely aligns with the conventional Aristotelian-Scholastic view that the irascible and concupiscible parts are distinct aspects of the soul, each governing different emotional and appetitive functions. --- ### II.7a.71 Conclusion : *Quae sit distinctio inter potentias animae cognoscitivas.* Translation : *What is the distinction between the cognitive powers of the soul.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Quae – What - sit – is - distinctio – distinction - inter – between - potentias – powers - animae – of the soul - cognoscitivas – cognitive (adjective modifying *potentias*) This is an interrogative asking about the differences between the soul's cognitive faculties. Philosophical Context : Pico is delving into the structure of the soul's intellect , possibly exploring different cognitive functions such as understanding, reason, and knowledge acquisition . This aligns with Neoplatonic and Aristotelian ideas about the multiplicity of intellectual faculties within the soul. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original in its specific inquiry but draws on established philosophical discussions about the complexity of the soul's cognitive abilities . Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion likely supports the conventional view that the soul possesses multiple cognitive powers , each responsible for different aspects of understanding and reasoning, consistent with Scholastic and Neoplatonic thought. --- ### II.7a.72 Conclusion : *Cur homines naturaliter appetant victoriam.* Translation : *Why do human beings naturally desire victory.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Cur – Why - homines – human beings - naturaliter – naturally - appetant – desire - victoriam – victory (accusative singular of *victoria*) This is an interrogative questioning the natural inclination of humans towards desiring victory. Philosophical Context : Pico is investigating the psychological and ethical motivations behind the human desire for victory. This could relate to discussions on virtue , honor , and achievement in Aristotelian and Scholastic ethics, as well as Neoplatonic notions of striving towards the Good. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original , reflecting Pico's personal exploration into human motivations, though it builds upon established philosophical themes regarding desire and virtue . Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion likely aligns with the conventional Aristotelian view that humans have an innate desire for excellence and honor , which are essential for the development of virtue and the fulfillment of their telos (purpose). --- ### II.7a.73 Conclusion : *Cur naturalis sit homini modus cognoscendi per rationem phantasiae coniunctam.* Translation : *Why is it natural for humans to know by a mode combining reason and imagination.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Cur – Why - naturalis – natural - sit – is - homini – to humans - modus cognoscendi – mode of knowing - per – by - rationem – reason - phantasiae – imagination (genitive singular of *phantasia*) - coniunctam – combined (accusative feminine singular of *coniunctus*) This is an interrogative exploring the natural cognitive process that integrates reason and imagination. Philosophical Context : Pico is examining the interplay between rational thought and imaginative faculties in human cognition. This reflects Neoplatonic and Renaissance humanist ideas that value both logical reasoning and creative imagination as essential for comprehensive understanding and innovation. Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original , demonstrating Pico's unique synthesis of reason and imagination within his epistemological framework, though it is inspired by broader philosophical traditions. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion likely supports the conventional view that a combination of reason and imagination is natural and necessary for human cognition, aligning with Scholastic and Neoplatonic beliefs that both faculties are essential for attaining truth and knowledge . --- ### II.7a.74 Conclusion : *Utrum in caelo sint descripta et significata omnia cuilibet scienti legere.* Translation : *Whether everything is written and signified in the heavens for anyone who knows how to read.* Vocabulary and Grammatical Features : - Utrum – Whether - in caelo – in the heavens - sint – is (subjunctive mood) - descripta – written (neuter plural participle of *describo*) - et – and - significata – signified (neuter plural participle of *significo*) - omnia – everything (neuter plural accusative) - cuilibet – by anyone - scienti – knowing (ablative singular of *scientia*) - legere – to read This is a yes/no interrogative questioning whether the heavens contain a coded or symbolic representation of all things, accessible through knowledge. Philosophical Context : Pico is exploring the idea of celestial symbolism —that the heavens (stars, planets, celestial events) contain a divine or metaphysical code that can be interpreted by those with the requisite knowledge. This aligns with Hermetic and Kabbalistic traditions, which view the cosmos as a text imbued with hidden meanings . Originality or Paraphrase : The question is original , reflecting Pico's unique integration of Neoplatonic , Hermetic , and Kabbalistic ideas into his philosophical system. Evaluation of Closeness to Conventional View : Pico's conclusion aligns with esoteric traditions that perceive the cosmos as a symbolic language meant to be deciphered by the knowledgeable. However, this perspective extends beyond the more conventional Scholastic view, which typically emphasizes more straightforward theological and philosophical interpretations of the cosmos. --- ### Summary of Originality and Conventional Alignment Giovanni Pico della Mirandola’s conclusions from *II.7a.60* to *II.7a.74* showcase his intricate engagement with Neoplatonic , Aristotelian , Scholastic , and esoteric traditions . His inquiries reflect a deep philosophical curiosity about the nature of the soul, the role of reason and imagination, the symbolic significance of numbers, and the interpretative frameworks of Biblical texts. Originality : - Integration of Traditions : Pico uniquely synthesizes Neoplatonic metaphysics with Christian theology and Kabbalistic mysticism, creating a comprehensive metaphysical system . - Numerological Symbolism : His exploration of the symbolic meanings of numbers in relation to humanity and the cosmos demonstrates an innovative approach to metaphysical inquiry. - Celestial Interpretation : Pico's questions about the symbolic language of the heavens indicate an original fusion of Hermetic and Kabbalistic ideas with philosophical analysis. Conventional Alignment : - Scholastic Foundations : Many of Pico's conclusions align with traditional Scholastic views, especially those derived from Aristotelian psychology and Neoplatonic metaphysics. - Rational and Imaginative Faculties : His emphasis on the combination of reason and imagination in cognition reflects conventional Renaissance humanist thought, which valued both faculties for comprehensive understanding. - Soul's Nature : Pico's inquiries into the immateriality and incorruptibility of the soul are consistent with Scholastic and Neoplatonic positions that uphold the soul's eternal and unchangeable nature. Philosophical Contribution : Pico's work stands out for its ambitious synthesis of various philosophical and theological traditions. By posing original questions that intertwine metaphysical principles with theological doctrines, he pushes the boundaries of conventional thought, inviting deeper exploration into the symbolism of numbers , the multifaceted nature of the soul , and the hidden meanings embedded within the cosmos. His influence on later thinkers like Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa , John Dee , and the broader Christian Kabbalistic tradition underscores the lasting impact of his philosophical endeavors. Pico's ability to bridge ancient philosophies with Renaissance humanism and esoteric traditions positions him as a pivotal figure in the development of modern metaphysical and mystical thought . ---

Write an essay on Pico's intervention in the tradition of Christian Platonism placing him in the context of Aquinas and Pseudo-Dionysius. Include a discussion of this text. Of Being and Unity (De Ente et Uno) By Giovanni Pico della Mirandola translated by Victor Michael Hamm, 1943.1 NOTES: 1. In this translation I have used the text of the 1557 (Venice) edition of Pico's Opera Omnia, as reprinted by Festugière in Archives d'Histoire Doctrinale et Littéraire du Moyen Age Vol. VII (1932), pp. 208-224, which text I have compared with that of the 1519 edition, available to me at the Newberry Library, Chicago. I have been aided by Father Festugière's French version of the Latin original, as well as by his notes. I have accepted Festugière's emendations of the text. To Angelo Poliziano PREFACE You were telling me lately of the dispute which you and Lorenzo de' Medici had concerning being and unity, and how, taking his stand with the Platonists, that man of a genius so powerful and versatile that he seems made for all things, who finds (wonderful to relate!) even in the incessant occupations of the State leisure for some literary study or conversation, argued against Aristotle, whose Ethics you expounded publicly this year. And since those who estrange Aristotle from Plato estrange themselves also from my point of view -- for I hold to the concord of both systems --, you ask me how we might defend the Stagirite on this point and bring him into agreement with his master, Plato. I have told you what came into my mind at that time, and it was rather a confirmation of your own objections against Lorenzo than a contribution of anything new. But you are not content with that. Without waiting for the developments which will come to the subject in my future Concord of Plato and Aristotle,2 you beg me to run over for you now, in the shape of a brief commentary, those things which I told you in the presence of our friend Domenico Benevieni, so dear to us for his knowledge and his integrity. How can I refuse you? Especially in a literary matter like this, and in the case of a friend who is almost my self? Pardon me, nevertheless, if I risk at times to employ words which perhaps have not yet received the stamp of true Latinity. The novelty of the subject, and I might almost say necessity, have demanded this license. Do not then expect a style too elegant and chaste. As our Malius3 says, the subject itself needs no ornament; simple exposition is enough. The following, therefore, if I remember well, were the things about which we had a discussion. 2. This was the projected work left unwritten because of Pico's early death. 3. "Malius" cannot be identified. Chapter I. For the Neo-Platonists Unity precedes Being. In more than one place Aristotle says that unity and being are convertible and reciprocal (the same is true of truth and goodness, but we shall speak of these later). This the followers of the Academy4 denied, saying that the one is anterior to being; by which they meant that they regarded the former as a concept more simple and universal. Wherefore they would define even God, the Sovereign Simplicity, as the One rather than as Being. Similarly, say they, prime matter, that crude and formless matter which is found in all things, ought to be included under the category of the one, and therefore they would exclude it from the category of being. Then, they say, unity and being have not the same opposites: to being is opposed non-being, to the one, the many. By the same law, therefore, by which their opposites are reckoned as two, being and unity are to be considered non-convertible and non-reciprocal. 4. Pico means especially the Neo-Platonists Plotinus and Proclus. Cf. L. Robin La théorie platonicienne des Idées et der Nombres d'après Aristote, (Paris 1908), passim: E. Brehier, Les idées philosophiques et religieuses de Philon d'Alexandrie, (Paris, 1908), 71 ff. Chapter II. Plato nowhere says that the one is superior to being, but rather that the two are equal. Such are the reasons they rest upon. Before we refute them, it would not be impertinent to find out what Plato himself thought on this point. I discover that he treats twice of being and unity: namely, in the Paramenides and in the Sophist. In these two places, therefore, according to the Academy, Plato gives the one priority over being. I shall say at once, as regards the Parmenides, that in this entire dialogue one does not find a single strict affirmation,5 and that, in any case, even if there were such an affirmation, nothing would allow one to draw such an inference with certitude. Actually there is nothing less dogmatic than this hook, which, taken in its totality, is nothing else than a sort of exercise in dialectic.6 Indeed, so far are the words of this dialogue from being opposed to my opinion, that all the attempts of critics to read something else into them achieve only arbitrary and willful interpretations. But let us dismiss all the critics. Let us instead inquire into the argument of the dialogue itself, and examine its beginning and its development, its promises and its performance. 5. Cf. Dié, ed. Parmenides, pp. 46: 'L'argumentation de Parmenide est donnée comme un jeu laborieux. Les Neo-platoniciennes, qui prennent 'laborieux' au sens de 'sérieux' . . . ont tiré de ce jeu toute une argumentation.' Cornford, Plato and Parmenides, (London, 1939), p. vii, writes: 'The conviction that Plato's purpose was serious and not merely destructive grows, the more clearly the Hypotheses are studied. If it is justified, the theory of the humorous polemic falls to the ground. 6. Cf. Cornford, op. cit., 131: 'What Parmenides offered Socrates was a gymnastic exercise, not the disclosure of a supreme divinity.' Here, then, is the content of the Parmenides: The discussion having started with the question whether all things that exist are one or many,7 Socrates turned it in the direction of the problem of ideas and overwhelmed Parmenides with questions on that subject,8 whereupon the latter exclaimed how he admired that transport, that energy of mind, which drove Socrates on to the definition of the highest truths.9 'Exercise yourself,' -- these are Parmenides' words -- 'train yourself thoroughly in this gymnastic while you are still young. Many will call it vanity, and accuse you of trifling and prating; yet if ever you cease from it, truth will escape you.'10 Everyone recognizes, -- and what follows makes it plain -- that Parmenides is here referring to dialectic. 7. Parmenides, 127d-130a. 8. Ibid., 130b-135c. Actually it is Parmenides, not Socrates, who directs the interrogation. 9. Ibid., 135d 2-3. 10. Ibid., 135d 3-5. Pico's translation of Plato's words follows that of Marsilio Ficino pretty closely. Cf. Ficino, Divini Platonis Opera Omnia, (Lugd., 1588), 46: 'Caeterum collige teipsum, diligentiusque te in ea facultate exerce, quae inutilis esse videtur multis, et quaedam garrulitas nuncupatur, dum iuvenis es, alioquin te veritas fugiet.' Thereupon, a propos of a new query of Socrates -- 'But in what, Parmenides, does this gymnastic consist?' -- the sage answered by first referring him to Zeno's argument as his model. Then, passing on to a more particular instruction, he with ingenious subtlety invites his adversary to consider not only what would follow from the existence of an object, but also what would follow from its non-existence; for example, in the case of this thing (the one) of which we posit or deny the existence, one must inquire what would follow both as regards the thing in itself, and in regard to other things, and, as regards other things, both in themselves and in respect to the one.11 While he is preparing to develop these points, Socrates cries out: "What a difficult task you set me there! I do not completely understand. But why do you not demonstrate this method which you vaunt so highly, by giving me a model on some point? I should then understand it better." Parmenides replies that this would be a great labor for a man of his advanced years. Thereupon Zeno insists that Parmenides ought to speak because the assembly is not numerous; if it were, the case would be different, for it is not becoming that an old man treat of such matters before a large public, since few people understand that it is necessary to consider questions so discursively in order to attain the truth.12 11. Pico is here translating the Greek text, 136a 3, in a somewhat too condensed form. 12. Parmenides, 136c 6-e 1. These words of Zeno fully confirm what we have said concerning the nature of the subject which Parmenides is going to treat. They do so at any rate if one agrees with Zeno that 'it is not becoming that an old man treat of such matters before a large public.' If, as some pretend,13 it were a question here of the divine hierarchies, of the first principle of all things, what discourse could we imagine more appropriate to an old man, or less calculated to make him blush? But it is beyond all dispute (unless we want to deceive ourselves) that Parmenides' subject is the dialectic method; besides, Socrates had demanded nothing else. Now, it is precisely such a subject which is, to Zeno, appropriate to a young man rather than to an old one. But for those who want other proofs, let us run through this dialogue. We shall nowhere find any dogmatic assertion, but everywhere only this question: 'If this is, what follows, and what if this is not?' 13. i.e. Proclus and the symbolists. The Academy, however, has taken occasion to defend its doctrine regarding being and unity because, in the first hypothesis,14 where he attacks the problem: if all things are one, what follows? Parmenides answers that that one of which existence is posited would be without parts, limitless, and therefore would be nothing;15 among many conclusions of this kind, he brings up this: 'that sort of one would not be being.'16 But is this not a mere exercise in dialectic? Is it really a dogmatic discourse on unity and being? There is a great difference between these two assertions: 'the one is above being,' and 'if all things are one, that one is not being.' But enough of the Parmenides. 14. 'if the One is one,' 137c-142b. 15. 137c-138b 6. 16. 141d 8-142a 8. As regards the Sophist, Plato there rather indicates the equivalence of unity and being17 than the priority of the one over being. Nowhere, indeed, do I find him speaking of priority, whereas there is an abundance of texts indicating equivalence. Take for example this passage: 'considering the question thus, you will confess that to say "something" is to say "some one thing;" and soon after: "He who says "not something" necessarily says "not some one thing," that is, he says nothing." '18 17. "Esse unum et ens aequalia" (Mirandula). 18. Sophist, 237d-e. Thus Plato. Not-one and nothing are therefore for him the same, rather, identical. Then the one and something are equal. After this he proves in the same way that it is impossible to say that not-being is one, and concludes thus: 'Being cannot be coupled with non-being;19 therefore unity cannot be coupled with non-being.'20 Now, he is speaking here of the unity which he had already called equal to that which is something. It seems then that he holds the identity of being and unity to be beyond doubt. 19. 238a 7. 20. 238c 4-7. Very well. We may agree that Plato arrived at that affirmation, though we do not find it explicitly stated in any of his writings. Let us see, then, in what sense it might have been so stated. And first of all let us discuss in these terms the foundations of the doctrine of Aristotle.21 21. Cf. Cornford, op. cit., 110-111: "It was from the Parmenides and from countless discussions to which it must have given rise that Aristotle learnt the maxim he so often repeats: 'One' and 'being' are used in many senses. But whereas Aristotle as a rule sets out with a systematic enumeration of the meanings of ambiguous terms, Plato makes his point by indirect procedure. . . . As we proceed, we shall find that Plato, in scattered passages, unobtrusively indicates the many ambiguities lurking in the phrase: 'If a One (or the One) is.' . . . Owing to certain peculiarities of Greek grammar, 'the one' (to en) can mean (1) Unity or Oneness in general; (2) the unity of anything that has unity or is one thing; (3) that which has unity, anything that is one; (4) the one thing we are speaking of as opposed to 'other ones,' and so on. The words for 'being' (to on, einai, ousia) are even more ambiguous, 'Being' can mean (1) the sort of being that belongs to any entity, whether it exists or not; (2) an entity which has being in this sense, any term that can be the subject of a true statement (3) the essence or nature of a thing; (4) existence; (5) that which has existence, or (collectively) all that exists." Chapter III. From the testimony of Parmenides, of Dionysius, and of Simplicius, we conclude the convertibility of unity and being. This word being, concerning which there is doubt whether it is equal to the concept unity, can be taken in two senses. The first is this: When we say 'being' we may mean anything that is outside of nothing. This is the sense of the word with Aristotle, wherever he makes being equivalent to unity. And this meaning is not unreasonable, for, as it is truly said, we ought to think with the few, but talk like the many. We think and judge for ourselves; we speak for the multitude, and we speak so that we may be understood. The vulgar, then, the unsophisticated, so understand being that they call anything 'being' (ens) which does not lack existence (esse), and which cannot properly be called nothing. But do we not find that those who are considered the wisest among the opposition22 have not understood being in any other way? 22. I.e. the Neo-Platonists who make unity superior to being. When Parmenides the Pythagorean said that the one is that which is, he meant God, if we credit Simplicius23 and all the many others who want to defend Parmenides against those who falsely accuse him of saying that all things are one.24 For they all agree in answering that, in employing the word 'one', Parmenides never believed that division, multiplicity, and plurality do not attach to things, since in other passages of his poems he himself openly affirms the contrary. But, they say, when he said 'one' what he meant is that to which the name of being truly applies, and which is truly the one being (esse), which one is God. Thus, for Parmenides and his defenders, even the 'Platonists,' the one cannot be above being unless it be above God. However, far from denying that God is being, it is to God alone that Parmenides accords, as is in truth fitting, the name of being. And so we solve the first difficulty of the 'Platonists.' 23. Simplicius, In Phys., (ed. H. Diels, Berlin, 1882) t. I, p.147, 12. 24. The universe -- a pantheistic interpretation, the only legitimate one, Cf. Cornford, op. cit., 29: "This One Being is not a mere abstraction; it proves to be a single continuous and homogeneous substance filling the whole of space." As regards Dionysius the Areopagite,25 whom our opponents invoke in favor of their opinion, he will not deny that God verily said to Moses: 'I am who am,' which reads in Greek: egw eimi o wn, that is, 'I am being' (ens). Of a truth, they themselves, when they say that nothing, or non-being is opposed to being as the many to the one, concede that of necessity that which is not being is nothing or non-being, in just the same way that what is not one is multiple or plurality. However, if they observe the same manner of speaking, they must say either that God is nothing, which appalls the ears, or that He is being. But to understand being in this fashion is to return to that which we have established as the first axiom and universal proposition, namely, that concerning anything it is necessary to say that it is or is not, and that concerning anything it is impossible to say or think both together at the same time. Since, therefore, outside of everything there is nothing but nothingness itself, if being understood in this sense excludes nothing but only nothingess, it is evidently necessary that being encompass all that is. Therefore the extension of unity cannot be greater unless it included nothingness itself, a possibility which Plato denies in the Sophist when he says that non-being or nothing cannot be called one. Besides, if unity cannot have less extension than being, it follows that being and unity must be convertible concepts. 25. Cf. The Divine Names, I, paragraph 6 (Patrologia Graeca, vol. III, 596 A-B). A convenient English translation of this work as well as of the Mystical Theology may be found in C. E. Rolt, Dionysius the Areopagite, (Macmillan, 1920). Chapter IV. In what sense one can say that something is superior to being. We have explained one of the senses which we said could be given to being. Understanding it so, -- a perfectly legitimate usage of the word -- one affirms most truly that there is nothing more common than being. It remains to explain the second sense, according to which it will be manifest that one can no less justly say that there is something that surpasses in eminence being itself. Words are either concrete or abstract. Concrete are, for example, hot, bright, white, man; abstract: heat, light, whiteness, humanity. This is their power and diversity: that what is called abstract connotes that which is such by itself (a se), not by another (ab alio), while concrete signifies that which is what it is not by itself, but by virtue of another. Thus the luminous is such in virtue of light, the white is such through whiteness, and man is man by virtue of his humanity. Moreover, since there is nothing which participates in itself, and since the same thing cannot possess the same quality at the same time by itself (a se) and by participation in another thing, it follows that the abstract cannot take its denomination from the concrete. Wherefore it is incongruous to say that whiteness is white, blackness black. Indeed, it is ridiculous to speak thus, not because whiteness is black or heat cold, but because such is the distance of the one from blackness and of the other from coldness, that all that is white is so by participation in the first, and all that is hot is so by participation in the second. When, then, we refuse to attribute such or such qualities to such or such an object, it is either because that object does not possess them, for example in the expression "black is not white," or because we want to signify that it possesses them in a more excellent and more perfect way than we do: as when we deny that whiteness is white, not because it is black, but because it is whiteness itself. But let us return to the subject. The word 'being' (ens) has the aspect of a concrete word. For to say 'being' (ens) and to say 'that which is' (id quod est) is to say the same thing. The abstract of this would seem to be the word esse, in that one calls ens that which participates in esse, in the same way that one calls luminous that which participates in light.26 If we look at this meaning of being which we have thus defined, we shall have to deny being not only to that which is not, and to that which is nothing, but also to that which is so that it is that being (esse) itself which is of itself (ex se) and by itself (a se), and by participation in which all things are: just as we not only deny that that is hot which lacks heat, but also that which is heat itself. Now, such is God, the plenitude of all being, the sole being a se, and from Whom alone, without the intervention of any intermediary, all things have come to be. 26. The language here is Platonic. We have, therefore, the right to say that God is not being but is above being, and that there is something to being, namely God Himself. If we give to God the name of the One, it follows that we avow the one to be above being. However, in calling God the One, we do so less to indicate what He is than to show in what manner He is all that is, and how other beings are through Him, 'God is called the One,' says Denys, 'because He is in a unique way all things,'27 and again: 'God is called the one because He is the principle of all things, just as unity is the principle of all numbers.28 Wherefore if (as the Academy pretends) Plato, in the first hypothesis of the Parmenides, affirms that the one is superior to being, that one will be nothing else than God. They (the Academy), indeed, themselves recognize this, since they admit by universal consensus that Plato here treats of the first principle of all things.29 27. The Divine Names, I, paragraph 7. (P. G., III, 596 D). 28. Ibid., II, paragraphs 4 and 11; V, paragraph 6. 29. Plotinus, Ennead V, I, 8. The Parmenides of Plato distinguishes the First One, or the one in the proper sense of the word; the second, which he calls the One-Many; and the third, or 'One-and-Many.' The First One is for the Neo-Platonists God Himself. But, some will say, on this point at least Aristotle differs from Plato, for Aristotle never understands being as subordinate to the one and as not including God in its extension. Those who speak in this way have not read Aristotle, for he does this very thing, and much more plainly than Plato. In the sixth book of his Metaphysics30 he divides being into being-by-itself (per se) and being-by-accident (per accidens). When being-by-itself (per se) is divided into ten categories, there is no doubt on the part of good interpreters of the philosopher that God is not included under this being, since He is neither being-by-accident nor is He contained under any one of the ten classes into which being per se is divided. Nothing is more of a commonplace among the Peripatetics than that division of being into substance and accident. Since this is so, we understand being so that God is above being and not below it, as St. Thomas himself teaches in the first book of his Commentaries on the Theological Sentences.31 I shall add that certain Platonists do wrong in vaunting themselves as if they possessed a secret unknown to Aristotle, when they say that God has two proper appellations, namely, the One and the Good, as if the good and the one were superior to being. Just as we have demonstrated that it did not escape the Peripatetics in what sense God can be understood as superior to being, so we are able to show that it was particularly these two names, the Good and the One, that Aristotle gave to God. 30. Metaphysics, E, 2, 1026 ff. 31. Commentum in libros IV. Sententiarum Magistri Petri Lombardi I, dist. xix, q. 4, ad 2 and esp. dist. xxiv, q. I, a. 1. In the second book of the Metaphysics,32 after having treated of being in its totality and of separate minds,33 he asks finally (as if, after all the rest, he wanted to turn to the investigation of the attributes of God alone), if, besides the good which is in the universality of things as in an army, there were some separate good like the person of the chief of this army, and he answers that this good exists, and that it is God. Of this God, in the same chapter, he demonstrates the unity, citing in testimony of this, after strong arguments, the phrase of Homer; eiz koiranoV estw, eiV basileuV.34 Where then is his error? Where is Aristotle at odds with Plato? Wherein is he profane? Wherein does he fail to give God the honors which are due to Him? 32. Metaphysics, A. 33. This book of the Metaphysics first studies sensible substance in its elements and its structure (ch. 1 to 5), then incorporeal immobile substance (ch. 6 to 10). The complex problem of the different first movers is touched on in ch. 8. The comparison with the army occurs in ch. 10, 1075 a 11 ff. 34. 10, 1076 a 4. Cf. Iliad II, 204. Pico misquotes Aristotle, who quotes Homer correctly: ouk agaqon polukoiranih: eiV koiranoV estw. Chapter V. In which is shown why the Peripatetics attribute to God many qualities which the Platonists deny Him, and how one may ascend through four degrees even to the cloud which God inhabits.35 35. This entire chapter is inspired by the Mystical Theology of the Pseudo-Denys. Let us respond now to the arguments which the Platonists invoke to sustain against Aristotle -- not in the sense with which we agree, but absolutely speaking -- the superiority of the one over being. We have, I think, already answered adequately the first of these arguments by which God is considered one and nevertheless is not being, but it is worth the labor to pursue the discussion in order to show that not only with the Platonists and Peripatetics, who disagree with one another, but often in the same single writer, there can be, with respect to the divine attributes, many affirmations and many negations equally just. God is everything, and he is everything in the most eminent and perfect way. Now, He would not be this unless He included in Himself all perfections in such a manner that He rejected all that has to do with imperfection in things. However, one must distinguish two kinds of imperfection. On the one hand, that is imperfect which in its class does not attain the perfection of that class or type. On the other, that is imperfect which, although perfect of its kind, is not absolutely perfect, because it has only the perfections of its kind, and there exist outside of it a number of kinds of things enriched with perfections that are proper to them and which, on its part, it does not include. As an example of the first case consider sense-knowledge, the imperfection of which comes not only from the fact that it is merely knowledge, and not appetition, but also from the fact that it is an imperfect kind of knowledge, both because of the organs which it must use, and which are brute and corporeal, and because it attains only to the superficial aspects of things and does not penetrate to the innermost reality, namely, the substance. So likewise is that human knowledge which one calls rational an imperfect knowledge, being vague, uncertain, mobile, and laborious. Even the intellectual knowledge36 of those divine intelligences called angels by the theologians, is nothing but an imperfect knowledge, if only because of the obligation it is under to seek without that which it does not possess within, at least in plenitude, namely, the light of truth which it needs for its actuation. 36. 'Intellectualem cognitionem,' (Mirandola) This is the classical distinction between discursive thought, logoV dianoia, and intuitive thought, nomsiV According to the Mystical Theology, ch. 3, It is necessary to pass beyond both before attaining God. Take another example: life. The life which resides in plants, indeed that which moves every body, is imperfect not only because it is life and not appetition, but because it is not pure life, but rather an influx of life derived from the soul in the body, constantly flowing, constantly mixed with death, fitter indeed to be called death than life. Are you unaware of it? We begin to die as soon as we begin to live, and death extends along with life, so that we stop dying only at that instant when corporeal death delivers us from the body of this death.37 But even the life of the angels is not perfect: unless the unifying ray of the Divine light incessantly vitalized it, it would slip completely into nothingness. So for all the rest. When therefore you make God knowing and living, attend first to this, that the life and knowledge which you ascribe to Him be understood as free from all these detriments. 37. Cf. I Corinthians XV, 31; Romans VII, 24. But this is not enough. There remains the second kind of imperfection, of which the following is an example.38 Imagine the most perfect kind of life possible, a life completely or perfectly alive, having in it nothing mortal, nothing mixed with death, a life which needs nothing outside or itself by means of which to remain stable and permanent. Imagine likewise a kind of knowledge which perceives everything at once and perfectly. Add this: that he who thus knows all things, knows them in himself and need not search outside himself the truth to be known, but be himself the very truth. Nay, to whatever high degree of perfection this life and this knowledge have attained in their proper natures, and though one could find them nowhere except in God, if, even in this degree of perfection one divides the one from the other, they are unworthy of God. 38. On this point cf. the Mystical Theology, chapters 4 and 5. For God, in short, is perfection in all its modes and in an infinite manner, but He is not such perfection merely because He comprehends in Himself all particular perfections and those in infinite number. For in that case, neither would He Himself be perfectly simple, nor would the perfections which are in Him be infinite; but He would be nothing more than a unique infinite, composed of many things infinite in number but finite in perfection.39 Now, to think or speak so of God is blasphemous. However, if the most perfect life possible is nonetheless only life, and not knowledge, and the same for all other similar perfections which are assembled in God, there will manifestly ensue a divine life of finite perfection, since it will have the perfection which pertains to life and not that which pertains to knowledge or to appetition. Let us then take from life not only that which makes life imperfect, but also that which makes it life merely, and do the same as regards knowledge and the other qualities which we have ascribed to God. Then what remains of all this will necessarily correspond to the idea which we want to have of God, namely, a Being one, absolutely perfect, infinite, altogether simple. And since life is a certain particular being, and wisdom likewise, and justice, if we remove from them this condition of particularity and limitation, that which remains will not be this or that being, but being itself, simple being, being universal, not with the universality of attribution but with the universality of perfection.40 Similarly wisdom is a particular good, because it is that good which is wisdom, and not that other which is justice. Take away, says St. Augustine,41 this, and take away that, that is to say, this limitation of particularity by which wisdom is that good called wisdom, and not that good called justice, and by which, similarly, justice has the particular goodness of justice and not that of wisdom; then only will you see in an obscure way42 the face of God, i.e. all good in itself, simple good, the good of all good. So also as life is a particular thing, it is one particular thing. For it is a certain perfection; and similarly wisdom is a certain perfection. Cast off the particularity, and there remains, not this or that unity, but the one itself, the absolute One. Since therefore God is that being which, as we said in the beginning, when the imperfections of all things are removed, is all things, certainly that which remains when you have rejected from all things both that imperfection which each one possesses in its kind, and that particularity which reduces each to one kind, will assuredly be God. God is, then, Being itself, the one Himself, the Good, and the True. 39. On this distinction between the quantitative infinite and the infinite of perfection, cf. St. Thomas, Summa Theologica, I, q. 7, a. 1 to 4. 40. That is to say, not abstract analogous being, but this concrete infinite being which is God. 41. Cf. Enarrationes in Psalmos, (Migue Patrologia Latina vols. XXXVI-XXXVII, 1490, 1741) in Ps. cxxxiv: "Dixit (Deus) Ego sum qui sum . . . non dixit Dominus Deus ille omnipotens, misericors, justus . . . Sublatis de medio omnibus quibus appellari posset et dici Deus, ipsum esse se vocari respondit et tamquam non esset ei nomen, hoc dices eis, inquit, qui est misit me." 42. 'In enigmate' (Mirandola). Cf. the Mystical Theology on this matter of the Divine darkness. In thus purifying the Divine names of all the stains that come from the imperfection of the things signified by them, we have already moved two steps in the ascent to the cloud which God inhabits. There remain two more, one of which indicates the deficiency of language, the other the weakness of our intelligence. These terms: being (ens), true, one, good, signify something concrete and as it were participated; wherefore we say again of God that He is being (esse) itself, truth itself, goodness itself, unity itself. Thus far indeed we are in the light, but God has placed His dwelling in the shadows.43 We have then not yet come to God himself. So long, in short, as that which we say of God is fully understood and entirely comprehended, we are in the light. But all that we say and perceive thus is a mere trifle, considering the infinite distance which separates Divinity from the capacity of our minds. In climbing to the fourth step we enter into the light of ignorance,44 and, blinded by the cloud of the Divine splendor, we cry out with the Prophet: 'I have fainted in Thy halls, O Lord,'45 finally declaring this one thing about God, that He is incomprehensibly and ineffably above all that we can speak or think of most perfect, placing Him pre-eminently above that unity and that goodness and that truth which we had conceived, and above being (esse) itself. Thus Denys the Areopagite, when he had written his Symbolic Theology, his Theological Institutes,46 the treatise on the Divine Names, and the Mystical Theology, and come to the end of the last-mentioned work, like a man already, so to speak, standing in the darkness and trying to find words most adequate to God, after some essays exclaimed: 'He is neither truth nor kingdom, nor unity, nor divinity,47 nor goodness, nor spirit, as we know it; one cannot apply to Him the names of son or of father or of any other things in the world known to us or to any other being. He is nothing of that which is not, nothing of that which is. Things which are do not know Him as He is, nor does He know things as they are.48 of Him there is no definition, neither is there a name nor a science of Him. He is neither darkness nor light, neither error nor truth,49 in short, every affirmation and every negation in regard to Him is equally impossible.' This is how that divine man expresses it. 43. Psal. XVII, 12: 'Et posuit tenebras latibulum suum, in circuitu ejus tabernaculum; tenebrosa aqua nubibus aeris.' 44. Cf. the 'superessential light of the Divine darkness' in the Mystical Theology, ch. I, paragraph 1, and the De Docta Ignorantia of Nicholas Cusanus. The latter work is available in a new critical edition by E. Hoffmann and R. Klibansky (Leipzig, 1932). 45. Psal. LXXXIII. 3: 'Deficit anima mea in atria Domini.' 46. I.e. the Hypotyposes theologicae. Cf. the Mystical Theology, ch. 3, where all these works are named. 47. Cf. C. E. Rolt's note (Dionysius the Areopagite, N.Y., 1920, p. 200, No. 2): "Godhead (divinity) is regarded as the property of deified men, and so belongs to relativity." 48. Cf. Rolt (ibid., 200, No. 3): "It (God) knows only Itself, and there knows all things in their Super-Essence-sub specie aeternitatis." 49. Rolt (200, No. 4): "Truth is an object of thought. Therefore, being beyond objectivity, the ultimate Reality is not Truth. But still less is it Error." I should rather say that truth is a relation or quality, and that since God transcends all relationship and all quality, He is not truth but THE TRUE. Let us gather up our conclusions. We learn, then, in the first degree, that God is not body, as the Epicureans say, nor the form of a body, as those say who affirm that God is the soul of the sky and of the universe -- the opinion of the Egyptians, according to the testimony of Plutarch50 and Varro the Roman theologian,51 whence they draw great nourishment for idolatry. Yet there are some even among the Peripatetics52 so stupid as to hold this the true doctrine and moreover as the teaching of Aristotle. How far they are from knowing God truly! They rest in the starting-place as if they had already reached the goal, and believe themselves already come to the heights of the Divinity while in fact they are lying on the ground and have not even begun to move a foot towards Him. For from this point of view God could be neither perfect life nor perfect being nor even perfect intelligence. But we have elaborately confuted these profane opinions in the fifth section of our Concordia.53 50. De Iside et Osiride, 49. Osiris is the nouV of the world-soul, Typhon its paqhtikon, seat of the passions. 51. De lingua latina, V, 10. 52. Allusion to the Averroist school at Padua. 53.
